interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Co-founder of Habitat for Humanity International and the Fuller Center for Housing
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Linda Fuller Degelmann, born in 1941 in Birmingham, Alabama, and raised in Tuscaloosa, recounts her journey from a segregated Southern white middle-class upbringing into a life devoted to housing ministry rooted in Christian faith. She describes her father's appliance business in Tuscaloosa (Caldwell Electric Company on Broad Street) and her early childhood awareness of segregation, including noticing that the family's Black employee was never invited to her parents' annual picnic. After marrying Millard (Dean) Fuller in 1959, the couple moved to Montgomery, Alabama, where Millard and his law partner Morris Dees built a marketing business (Fuller & Dees Marketing) while attending Alabama Klan rallies in support of MacDonald Gallion's lieutenant governor campaign and subscribing to The Fiery Cross publication. Linda describes how Millard and Morris witnessed the brutal beatings of Freedom Riders at the Montgomery bus station, prompting a moral shift; the pair then cancelled their Fiery Cross subscription in a confrontation in which Morris placed a pistol on the desk, after which their offices were broken into and vandalized.

Linda describes hosting marchers during the Selma to Montgomery March (1965), drawing nasty phone calls and license-plate surveillance from neighbors. A 1965 marriage crisis led the Fullers to give away their wealth, sell Millard's interest to Morris Dees (who later founded the Southern Poverty Law Center), and seek God-directed work. Through Dr. Durgan at Broadway United Church of Christ, they raised money for a dormitory at Tougaloo College while living in New Jersey for two years. The couple then met Clarence Jordan at Koinonia Farm in December 1965 and moved there in summer 1968, where they helped develop the Koinonia Partnership idea of partnership housing, partnership industries (pecan plant, sewing, pottery), and partnership farming, founding a child development center.

Linda recounts a harrowing arrest in Unadilla, Georgia, when she, Millard, and their daughter Faith visited a Black family supporting a teenage son who wanted to integrate the white high school; a policeman threatened them and after release at the city jail they were stalked to a motel before a county sheriff intervened with FBI contact. She describes a 1965 incident at First Baptist Church in Americus, when Clarence Jordan, Collins McGee (a Black resident of Koinonia), and roughly twenty others were ejected by the head deacon (also the town's fire chief) after attending a service to which the Georgia Baptist Convention head had invited them. After three years building 120 houses in what was then Zaire (formerly the Congo), the Fullers returned to Sumter County and founded Habitat for Humanity International, which they led until 2005, building houses for more than a million people across 100 countries. Jimmy and Rosalynn Carter joined the work in 1984. In 2005 the Fullers founded the Fuller Center for Housing. Linda also recounts Millard's later civil rights legal work including death penalty defense and integration of local law enforcement following a 1971 Black boycott and march in Americus.
key_themes: Christian faith as foundation for civil rights and economic justice work
Personal transformation from Southern segregationist culture to integrationist activism
Voluntary divestment of wealth and partnership economics
Housing as a vehicle for racial and economic justice
Family and community costs of crossing racial lines in the segregated South
Movement from local Southern racial justice work to international anti-poverty ministry
Resistance and retaliation faced by Koinonia Farm and integrationist white Southerners
main_summary.historical_significance: Linda Fuller Degelmann's interview documents the intersection of the Civil Rights Movement with the Christian intentional-community movement at Koinonia Farm (where the partnership housing concept that became Habitat for Humanity originated) and provides firsthand testimony of the social repercussions faced by white Southerners who allied with the movement in Montgomery and southwest Georgia in the 1960s. Her account adds texture to the documented history of the Selma to Montgomery March, the Freedom Rides aftermath in Montgomery, and the integration of churches and law enforcement in rural Sumter County, Georgia. The interview also preserves a co-founder's perspective on the origin of Habitat for Humanity International, an institution whose roots in the civil rights-era partnership economics of Clarence Jordan's Koinonia Farm are often understudied in mainstream accounts.

errors: Summary states Habitat was founded after returning from Africa with three years there; transcript confirms 'three years in Africa' but does not give a precise founding year for Habitat for Humanity. The summary should not embellish a specific year.

Summary mentions Habitat 'until 2005' and Carter joining 'in 1984'; both dates are stated by Linda in the transcript (segments 895, 896, 1115) and are supported, but the summary's framing as 'led until 2005' should be cross-checked against transcript wording 'right before we left habitat in 2005'.

Summary attributes 'lieutenant governor campaign' to MacDonald Gallion; transcript says they spoke on behalf of 'MacDonald Gallion' running for Lieutenant Governor (segments 97-98), but does not provide a date for this campaign, so the framing is faithful to transcript.
improvements: Tighten language around the 1965 Selma to Montgomery March hosting episode to make clear Linda did not march herself (segment 132).
Clarify that the Unadilla incident's exact year is not given in the transcript; the summary should not imply a precise date.

Note that the transcript identifies the Fuller daughter who made the documentary 'Briars in the Cotton Patch' as Faith Fuller; verify if the documentary was released 2003 (segment 452) before stating in summary.

chapters.title: Childhood and Family Background in Tuscaloosa, Alabama
chapters.summary: Linda Fuller Degelmann describes her birth in 1941 in Birmingham, Alabama, her father's service in the Army Reserves at the procurement office in Philadelphia during World War II, and the family's move to Tuscaloosa, Alabama after the war. Her parents started an appliance business called Caldwell Electric Company on Broad Street in downtown Tuscaloosa. The family was among the first in the city to own a dishwasher and one of the first to have a television. Linda recalls observing as a child that a Black man who worked in her father's repair shop delivering appliances was never invited to her parents' annual picnic cookout, and her early questions about this exclusion were dismissed. She attended segregated schools and notes that the University of Alabama was not yet integrated during her childhood.
related_events: World War II Army Reserve mobilization
Southern segregation in domestic and business life

improvements: Could note that the family had one older sister and that Linda's family did not have direct activism in this chapter.

chapters.title: Marriage to Millard Fuller and Awakening in Montgomery
chapters.summary: Linda describes meeting and marrying Millard (Dean) Fuller in 1959. She credits Millard's exposure to integrated youth conferences in the North, including being greeted by a Black woman, as the source of his early racial-justice perspective. After moving to Montgomery, Alabama, where Millard and his law-school partner Morris Dees set up a marketing business and law practice, she describes pivotal events: Millard and Morris went to the Montgomery bus station to witness Freedom Riders being beaten as they stepped off buses. The pair, while not Klan members, had spoken at Klan rallies in support of MacDonald Gallion's run for Lieutenant Governor and subscribed to the Klan publication The Fiery Cross. After the Freedom Ride violence shocked them, they called a Klan leader into their office to cancel their subscription, with Morris placing a pistol on his desk; within days their offices at Fuller & Dees Marketing were broken into and vandalized.
related_events: Montgomery bus station attacks on Freedom Riders

improvements: Could note Linda's own role was secondhand observer here, not direct participant.

chapters.title: Hosting Selma to Montgomery Marchers and Education at Huntingdon College
chapters.summary: Linda recounts that during the Selma to Montgomery March (1965), several pastors from Ohio or Indiana whom Millard knew came down to participate and stayed at the Fuller home in Montgomery. Linda and Millard provided floor space for four to six marchers to sleep, and Millard and Morris drove them to the march route each morning. The family received nasty phone calls after a license-plate photograph of their car was taken at the march, and their neighbors stopped speaking to them. Linda notes that they did not personally participate in any marches but supported them. She describes her education at Huntingdon College in Montgomery (a then-predominantly-white college), which she attended from 1961 to 1966. She required a Black caregiver for her children whom she had to drive to the house because of the bus boycott. The college required Old and New Testament, ethics, and philosophy classes, which broadened her thinking, and she observes there were no Black students that she remembers at Huntingdon during her enrollment.
related_events: Bob Zellner / Albany Movement (referenced by interviewer)

improvements: Could note Linda's specific recollection of having young children during the march hosting, and her guilt about not being more involved with the marchers' meals (segments 205-208).

chapters.title: Marriage Crisis, Giving Away Wealth, and Tougaloo College Fundraising
chapters.summary: Linda describes the 1965 crisis in her marriage, brought on by Millard and Morris Dees's ambition to become millionaires by age 30 which required constant work. She left Millard, and through counseling at Broadway United Church of Christ with Dr. Durgan, they decided together to give away their wealth and let God direct their lives. They sold Millard's half-interest in Fuller & Dees Marketing to Morris Dees, who would later (five or six years afterward) found the Southern Poverty Law Center. Dr. Durgan, who was on the board of Tougaloo College, a predominantly Black college in Mississippi, recruited Millard to raise money for a new dormitory. The Fullers moved to New Jersey for two years (Montclair and Glen Ridge area), where Millard commuted into New York City to raise money. After the dormitory was funded, the family was called back south to Koinonia Farm to work with Clarence Jordan. Linda recounts a December 1965 month-long visit with Allen and Carol Henry at Koinonia where they first met Clarence Jordan; Allen Henry had been kicked out of his Birmingham, Alabama, church for proposing welcoming Black people.
related_events: Founding of Southern Poverty Law Center (1971)
Allen and Carol Henry's expulsion from Birmingham church

improvements: Could be more explicit that Linda's recollection of when Morris Dees founded SPLC is 'five or six years later' (segment 152), which she presents as approximate.

chapters.title: Moving to Koinonia Farm and the Partnership Housing Movement
chapters.summary: Linda describes moving to Koinonia Farm in the summer of 1968, when the community was at a low ebb with Clarence Jordan, his wife Florence, and one son still at home, and very few other residents. Clarence had been considering leaving to teach in a seminary. The Fullers wrote to Clarence asking what he had planned, and they met at a church in Atlanta where Clarence was preaching. A brainstorming weekend with pastors and business people produced the concept of the Koinonia Partnership, including partnership housing, partnership industries, and partnership farming. Linda describes the early houses (simple cinder-block construction with indoor bathrooms, running water, kitchen cabinets, and closets), with the Johnson family the first to move in. About ten houses were built on Koinonia's 2,000-acre tract, sold to families at no profit and no interest, which was the only way Black families could afford them since they could not get bank loans. Linda set up a child development center using her elementary-education degree. The partnership industries included a pecan plant, sewing, and pottery. Linda also recounts the history of Koinonia, founded by Clarence and Florence Jordan with Martin and Mabel England, as an intentional Christian community to live out the teachings of Jesus from the Greek New Testament, in defiance of the segregated culture Clarence had grown up in in Talbotton, Georgia. Linda describes the violent history of Koinonia in the 1950s, including drive-by shootings, bullet holes in houses (one narrowly missing a Jordan daughter), Black families threatened with house burnings, and Conrad Browne being arrested for sharing a bag of peanuts with a Black Koinonia resident walking down the street.
related_events: Clarence Jordan's Greek New Testament scholarship

improvements: Could clarify that Koinonia's actual founding year (1942) is not given in the transcript; Linda only refers generally to 'back in the forties' (segment 356).
Could note that Linda specifies Koinonia had 'about 2000 something acres' (segment 241), an approximate not exact.

chapters.title: Arrest in Unadilla and First Baptist Church Ejection in Americus
chapters.summary: Linda recounts two emblematic encounters with white-Southern resistance. In Unadilla, Georgia, about 30-40 miles east of Americus across Interstate 75, a young Black man challenged the segregated school system by attempting to enroll in the white high school so he could become qualified for medical school. The Fullers and infant daughter Faith drove there with strawberries to support the family. While waiting on the family's porch, a policeman arrived with his gun drawn and arrested Millard (and then Linda voluntarily) for violating a 'city ordinance' against whites visiting with Black residents. After detention at the city hall jail and a meeting of the county commissioner, they were released with the warning that the police were 'not responsible' once they left town. The Fullers checked into a motel, saw a van of men pull in behind them, and Millard called local churches for help; the Episcopal church director refused, but the county sheriff (not on good terms with the police chief) came over and contacted the FBI before Clarence Jordan arrived from Koinonia to escort them home. Linda also describes a December 1965 visit to First Baptist Church in Americus, where the head of the Georgia Baptist Convention (Carol and Allen Henry's father) had invited their party to attend a Christmas service. About 20 people from Koinonia, including Clarence, Millard, Linda, and Collins McGee (the only Black person in the group), filed in; the head deacon, who was also the town fire chief, ejected Collins, prompting Clarence to lead the entire group out. Outside, Clarence remarked on the floodlit white columns of the church: 'It's all that light on the outside and it's dark on the inside.'

improvements: Distance from Americus to Unadilla is given as 'about 30, 40 miles east' (segment 427), which is approximate; map data suggests Unadilla is northeast across I-75.
Could quote Clarence Jordan's remark more precisely.

chapters.title: Africa Mission Work and the Founding of Habitat for Humanity
chapters.summary: Linda describes a six-week trip to five African countries (Tanzania, Rhodesia [now Zimbabwe], South Africa, Ghana, and the Congo) that Millard took at the request of the United Church of Christ, which had earlier offered him such a tour during his business years but which he had declined as too costly. The family then moved for three years to what was then Zaire (the former Belgian Congo, now Democratic Republic of Congo), where they pioneered the partnership-housing model in a city flooded with rural-to-urban migrants newly free from Belgian colonial movement controls. They built 120 houses over about two and a half years (with the first house taking a year and a half because of difficulties getting a concrete-block factory running). Linda notes their two oldest children attended an American school in Kinshasa. Upon return in 1976, the Fullers wanted to launch a worldwide ministry from Koinonia, but the new Koinonia leadership preferred to focus locally on Sumter County; the Fullers were given part of a chicken barn for an office, and the new organization was named Habitat for Humanity International. The family moved into town in Americus, where Millard opened a law office and Linda ran the Habitat phone with him as a 'mom and pop' operation. Linda also describes the local hostility they faced after moving into town (broken glass on the driveway, neighbors' children forbidden to play with theirs), the support from Habitat blitz-builds (10-20 houses built in a week), and the eventual economic and social acceptance of Habitat in Americus. Jimmy and Rosalynn Carter joined the work in 1984.
related_events: Founding of Habitat for Humanity International (1976)

improvements: The transcript states 'almost two years' in Africa (segment 273-275), but also mentions three years overall; the chapter should be clear about the discrepancy.
1976 is implied as return date (segment 705, daughter in 4th grade with Amy Carter at end of Carter campaign), not directly stated.

chapters.title: Habitat Growth, International Anti-Poverty Work, and Death Penalty Cases
chapters.summary: Linda describes the growth of Habitat from its 1970s Americus origins (raising about $70,000 in its first year) to a 100-country international organization with over 900 employees that, by the time the Fullers left in 2005, was receiving about a quarter of a million dollars or more per day. She recounts the philosophy of using appropriate technologies and local materials in each country, often staying with Habitat families in Sri Lanka, South Korea, and Fiji. Linda then turns to Millard's law work in Americus, including representing Black families denied employment in the all-white police and sheriff's department after a 1971 march and boycott in downtown Americus, in which Millard and Linda participated. Linda recounts how Millard helped calm an angry meeting in a Black church by encouraging Black applicants to apply formally (so that the inevitable rejection could be the basis for a legal case). She describes Millard's later involvement in death-penalty defense alongside a Black Atlanta lawyer (named in transcript as 'Millard Farmer'), discovering that prosecutors used the question of whether jurors believed in the death penalty to systematically exclude Black jurors, producing all-white juries. Linda recounts one strategy in which Millard would lead a hot afternoon courtroom in prayer to recapture juror attention. She also notes Morris Dees's Southern Poverty Law Center work integrating the Alabama state patrol and protecting victims of Klan and other 'subversive' groups.

errors: Transcript names the Black Atlanta death penalty lawyer as 'Millard Fuller Farmer' (segment 1016) which appears to be an ASR transcription artifact for 'Millard Farmer'. The summary uses 'Millard Farmer'; the transcript's exact garbled rendering should be acknowledged.
improvements: Could clarify that the 1971 date for the boycott is taken from the interviewer's biography reference (segment 997), not from Linda's own clear date statement.

chapters.title: Founding the Maranatha Congregation, Family Costs, and Family Influence
chapters.summary: Linda describes founding a small congregational church in their Montgomery home (around 20 people, eventually meeting in the Huntingdon College chapel) before founding a small building on the outskirts of Montgomery. Allen Henry served as a visiting minister before they hired a full-time pastor. Linda notes that after the Fullers left Montgomery, the church effectively died. Years later in Americus, Jimmy Carter persuaded the Fullers to join Maranatha Baptist Church, his church in Plains, Georgia. Linda also reflects on the personal costs of their racial-justice work: most of their friends in Montgomery shunned them, but they gained far more friends than they lost. Their families on both sides were 'disgusted' and 'disappointed,' some saying they needed to be in a 'mental institution' when they gave away their money. Her mother avoided controversial discussions, her father refused engagement, but Linda's sister, who had moved to California with her husband, was supportive. Linda also notes that Morris Dees's parallel trajectory after the Freedom Rides incident is similar to her family's, but they did not stay in close contact.
related_events: Morris Dees Southern Poverty Law Center

improvements: Could note that Linda's specific framing of Maranatha as 'Jimmy Carter twisted her arm' (segment 993) is a colorful but accurate Linda quote.

chapters.title: Reflections on the Civil Rights Movement and Family Legacy
chapters.summary: Linda reflects on how she sees the Civil Rights Movement as a 'template' for other freedom movements (women's, minority rights, mental-health awareness) that 'spurred so many other freedom movements,' showing that 'people could speak out and demonstrate another way and eventually get to people's hearts and minds.' She references hearing Andrew Young speak about the Birmingham Children's Crusade of May 1963, including the spontaneous moment when marchers sat down as a gesture of being humble, after which 'they eventually turned those hoses off.' She emphasizes the importance of meeting people on a 'real human level' across cultures, including how Millard would dance in African villages to defy the stereotype of stiff white Western dignitaries. Linda describes how their four children were shaped by Koinonia and the family's international experience, including their son's job leading children's activities in Saudi Aramco's expatriate community in Saudi Arabia and their daughter's career as a high-end home builder in Denton, Texas, who later led a Habitat house build. Linda also reflects that the 2005 founding of the Fuller Center for Housing continued the family's housing-ministry mission after the Fullers left Habitat.
related_events: Andrew Young's later civil rights career
Civil Rights Movement as template for other freedom movements

improvements: Linda's recollection of Andrew Young recounting the Birmingham Children's Crusade hose moment is a secondhand recollection; should be marked as such in the summary.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Co-founder of Habitat for Humanity International and the Fuller Center for Housing
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Linda begins with birth and family background then proceeds roughly chronologically through marriage, Montgomery, Koinonia, Africa, and Habitat (Segment 14, 00:01:02,000)
Clear transitions between chronological eras (Segment 308, 00:38:49,000) 'When we moved to Koinonia Farm in the summer of 1968...'
Some interviewer-prompted backtracking, e.g., interviewer asks 'Actually, let me back up' (Segment 83, 00:08:17,000) to address Freedom Rides
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a generally chronological arc from Linda's childhood through Habitat for Humanity's founding to family legacy, with clear interviewer-facilitated transitions. Some backtracking occurs to fill in earlier events (Freedom Rides, marriage crisis), but the overall organization is easy to follow.

most_vivid_moment.quote: He turned around and he looked at the church. Big white columns. And there were floodlights, you know, just beaming on this church building. And Clarence made the remark, just look at there. It's at all that light on the outside and it's dark on the inside.

evidence: Description of the Koinonia houses with cinder block and indoor plumbing (Segment 244, 00:33:29,000)
Strawberry-laden visit to Unadilla family and the policeman with gun drawn (Segment 594, 00:58:08,000)
Daughter Faith totaling around and playing notes on a piano in the jail (Segment 616, 00:59:59,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Linda provides numerous specific names (Millard Fuller, Morris Dees, Clarence Jordan, Florence Jordan, Allen Henry, Carol Henry, Collins McGee, Dr. Durgan, MacDonald Gallion, Gussie Jackson, Faith Fuller, Amy Carter, Jimmy Carter, Rosalynn Carter, Andrew Young, etc.), specific dates (1941, 1959, 1965, 1968, 1971, 1976, 1984, 2005), and specific places (Birmingham, Tuscaloosa, Montgomery, Americus, Unadilla, Talbotton, New Jersey, Tougaloo, Kinshasa). The Unadilla incident and First Baptist Church ejection are reconstructed in vivid detail with reconstructed dialogue.

most_authentic_moment.quote: Excuse me, but she told us she said, you know, I baked those cakes. She said, I had to show them that I was bigger than how I was being treated.

vulnerability_elements: Acknowledges marriage crisis and her leaving Millard (Segment 142, 00:14:50,000)
Acknowledges fear during the motel stalking (Segment 638, 01:01:28,000)
emotional_authenticity.justification: Linda shares the marriage crisis, her family's rejection of their integrationist work, and a number of frightening moments with genuine emotional honesty, though her demeanor remains measured and reflective rather than rawly emotional. She is willing to discuss difficult truths about her family's reaction without melodrama.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Linda's narrative quality is strong overall, with a clear chronological arc, vivid reconstructed scenes (especially the Unadilla arrest and First Baptist Church ejection), and genuine emotional honesty about family rupture and the costs of integration work. The interviewer's gentle backtracking creates some structural complexity but does not derail the overall coherence. The interview's storytelling is well-paced and supported by abundant proper nouns and specific dates.

major_events: Hosting Selma to Montgomery March participants (1965) at the Fuller home (Segment 121-129, 00:12:26,000)
First Baptist Church Americus integration attempt with Clarence Jordan (December 1965) (Segment 787-826, 01:14:03,000)

key_figures.name: Clarence Jordan
key_figures.relationship: co-resident at Koinonia Farm and mentor

key_figures.name: Andrew Young
key_figures.relationship: speaker she heard at a conference

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Co-founder of Habitat for Humanity with insider knowledge of the partnership-housing concept's origin at Koinonia Farm, with close personal relationship to Clarence Jordan and the Carters.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Linda was directly present at the originating moments of Habitat for Humanity and the Koinonia Partnership, and hosted Selma marchers in her Montgomery home. She has rare insider perspective on the intersection of the white Southern Christian community and the civil rights movement, though most of her Civil Rights Movement-specific exposure is at one remove (through Millard and Morris).

precision_examples: Married Millard Fuller in 1959 at age 18 (Segment 23/201, 00:02:15,000 / 00:26:07,000)
Founded Fuller Center for Housing in 2005 (Segment 1115, 02:07:21,000)
verifiable_claims: Habitat in 100 countries with 900+ employees by 2005
specificity_of_details.justification: Linda provides many verifiable dates and full names tied to organizational and biographical milestones, with good precision on years even when months and days are absent. A few claims are presented as approximate ('about 30, 40 miles east,' 'about 2000 something acres,' 'almost two years' in Africa).

new_information: Detailed origin of the partnership-housing concept at Koinonia Farm that became Habitat for Humanity, with the specific Johnson family as first beneficiaries
First-person account of white Southern social ostracism for hosting Selma marchers in Montgomery
Eyewitness recollection of First Baptist Church Americus's December 1965 ejection of Collins McGee from a Christmas service
Insight into Millard Fuller and Morris Dees's pre-Civil-Rights affiliations (Klan-adjacent activities and lieutenant governor campaign for MacDonald Gallion)
understudied_aspects: Christian intentional-community contributions to the Civil Rights Movement (Koinonia Farm)
Personal costs paid by white Southerners who joined the civil rights work
The local-Americus context of the late-1960s/early-1970s Black boycott and law-enforcement integration
Habitat for Humanity's roots in civil-rights-era partnership economics rather than just philanthropy
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Linda's account complicates the standard narrative that the Civil Rights Movement was primarily a Black-led effort by documenting parallel white Christian integrationist work, while also acknowledging that her family was 'mostly observers' and supporters rather than frontline activists.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of Habitat for Humanity, Koinonia Farm, Clarence Jordan, and the broader Christian wing of the southern Civil Rights Movement could use this interview to corroborate institutional history and gain personal-perspective texture on figures like Clarence Jordan, Florence Jordan, and Morris Dees in his pre-SPLC years.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: The interview makes a meaningful contribution to the documented history of Koinonia Farm, the partnership-housing concept, and the Christian wing of southern civil rights work. It is less novel as a Civil Rights Movement first-person account, since Linda repeatedly notes she was an observer rather than a direct participant in the major events of the era.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Linda's interview has strong historical value for the history of Habitat for Humanity, Koinonia Farm, and the Christian wing of the Civil Rights Movement, with abundant specific details and clear documentation of key institutional moments. As an oral history of the Civil Rights Movement proper, the interview is most valuable for its perspective on the social costs paid by white Southerners who allied with the movement.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Linda describes a personal arc from a sheltered, segregated upbringing in Tuscaloosa through a 1965 marriage crisis that prompted the Fullers to give away their wealth and pursue a God-directed life, culminating in international anti-poverty work and the founding of two housing-ministry organizations.
key_turning_points: Marriage crisis and decision to give away wealth in 1965 (Segment 139-145, 00:14:36,000)
Meeting Clarence Jordan at Koinonia Farm in December 1965 (Segment 781-784, 01:13:09,000)
Witnessing Freedom Rider beatings via Millard's account (Segment 87-95, 00:08:47,000)
Arrest in Unadilla and motel stalking (Segment 599-661, 00:58:47,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Linda contrasts her early life as a 'businessman's daughter' in a 13-room Montgomery house with the simple cinder-block partnership-housing life at Koinonia and later in Americus, calling the new lifestyle 'much richer.'
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: The arc from voluntary divestment to founding a worldwide housing ministry is genuinely inspirational, though Linda tells the story with characteristic Southern Christian reserve rather than self-promotion.
transformation_growth.justification: Linda articulates a clear before/after personal arc tied to specific turning points (1965 marriage crisis, 1965 meeting with Clarence, 1968 move to Koinonia). She does not, however, deeply explore her internal psychological experience of the transformation.

peak_moments.stakes: physical safety of the Fuller family including infant daughter

peak_moments.description: First Baptist Church Americus ejection of Collins McGee
peak_moments.stakes: racial integration of southern white churches and the public witness of Koinonia

peak_moments.description: Fuller & Dees offices vandalized after Klan publication cancellation
peak_moments.stakes: personal safety after breaking with the Klan's social orbit

peak_moments.description: 1971 Americus march down empty downtown streets with closed stores
peak_moments.stakes: Black employment rights and the threat of riot

conflict_types: moral conflict (Klan affiliation vs. emerging civil rights awareness)
institutional conflict (church segregation, law enforcement segregation)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From fear (motel stalking) to moral indignation (church ejection) to triumph (Habitat's growth to 100 countries)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Several dramatic moments are vividly described, especially the Unadilla arrest and the First Baptist Church ejection. The interview includes both physical-danger moments and longer-term institutional conflicts, though Linda's measured tone keeps the tension restrained.

universal_themes: transition from wealth to simple living
relatable_struggles: Marriage strain from a partner's all-consuming career ambition
Parental disappointment with adult children's life choices
Balancing childcare with personal education (Huntingdon College while raising young children)
Fear when entering unfamiliar or hostile communities
human_moments: Linda's mother saying 'Oh, no, no progress in three generations' on first visit to Koinonia (Segment 365, 00:42:13,000)
Linda holding her infant daughter at the Unadilla porch when the policeman drew his gun (Segment 600, 00:58:50,000)
Linda's anxiety about diaper supply in jail (Segment 611, 00:59:43,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Universal themes of marriage rupture and recommitment, family disagreement, and the cost of unconventional moral choices make the interview accessible to audiences who do not share Linda's specific religious or civil-rights context.
relatable_experiences.justification: The interview includes genuinely relatable moments (especially the family rejection, the diaper-shortage anxiety in the jail, and the mother's despairing remark) that ground the larger civil-rights and ministry narrative in everyday human experience. The relatability is moderate rather than exceptional because Linda's distinctive Southern Christian voluntary-poverty framework is somewhat removed from typical audience experience.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: The interview offers a moderately strong emotional arc grounded in a clear personal transformation, several genuinely dramatic moments (Unadilla arrest, First Baptist Church ejection), and accessible human moments (family rupture, parental disappointment, diaper anxiety in jail). The emotional reserve characteristic of Linda's Southern Christian voice keeps the tone measured rather than raw, somewhat capping the resonance score.

barriers: Some Christian terminology (e.g., 'God plan thing,' 'called') may require slight familiarity
References to specific local institutions (Koinonia Farm, Maranatha Baptist Church) without much initial explanation
language_clarity.justification: Linda speaks clearly with mild Southern dialect that should not impede comprehension for general audiences. Her Christian framework is articulated accessibly with occasional explanations of internal terminology.

slow_sections: Discussion of educational system specifics at Huntingdon College (00:22:00-00:24:00)
Lengthy explanation of Habitat blitz-build administrative details (01:23:00-01:27:00)
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: The interview sustains audience attention through ~2 hours with several dramatic peaks; some sections dealing with institutional development may lose general audience attention but are valuable to specialists.
narrative_flow.justification: The interviewer (Emily Crosby) facilitates well with thoughtful follow-up questions and gentle backtracking when something is glossed over. Linda's natural pacing is steady with strong rhythmic peaks during dramatic anecdotes.

themes_connected: Civil Rights Movement as template for other freedom movements
Christian faith as foundation for racial justice
Anti-poverty work as the natural continuation of civil rights work
Family and community costs of crossing racial lines
International dimension of American civil rights ethics
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Linda explicitly connects the civil rights movement to other freedom movements (women's, mental health, minority rights) and to ongoing housing-poverty work in 100 countries, making the historical significance accessible to contemporary audiences.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Linda explicitly reflects on the historical significance of her experiences and the broader meaning of the partnership-housing concept, especially in the final reflections section (01:59:00-02:06:00).
broader_themes_connection.justification: Linda's closing reflections (segments 1083-1106) explicitly tie her personal experience to the Civil Rights Movement's broader influence on other freedom movements and to the international anti-poverty work that followed, making the 'why does this matter' question fully accessible.

accessibility.overall_assessment: The interview is highly accessible: clear articulation, slight and easily-handled Southern dialect, minimal jargon, and explicit thematic connections to the broader Civil Rights Movement and contemporary freedom movements. The interviewer's facilitation and the natural narrative pacing sustain attention through the two-hour interview.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Linda Fuller Degelmann delivers a coherent, detail-rich oral history that spans her own life arc from a segregated Tuscaloosa childhood through co-founding Habitat for Humanity International to founding the Fuller Center for Housing in 2005. The interview's primary historical value is its first-person documentation of the origin of the partnership-housing concept at Koinonia Farm and its insider perspective on Clarence Jordan, Millard Fuller, and Morris Dees's parallel post-Freedom-Rides moral conversion. As a Civil Rights Movement first-person account it is most valuable for documenting the social costs paid by white Southerners who allied with the movement (hosting Selma marchers, the Unadilla arrest, the First Baptist Church ejection). The interview's accessibility, vivid reconstructed scenes, and explicit connections to broader freedom movements make it suitable for both general and specialist audiences.
key_strengths: Rare first-person account of the origin of Habitat for Humanity's partnership-housing concept at Koinonia Farm, with specific dates, names, and organizational details that historians can verify
Vivid reconstructed scenes (Unadilla arrest, First Baptist Church ejection, marriage crisis) that ground the larger institutional history in concrete human moments
Explicit reflection on the personal and family costs of integrationist work for white Southerners, which is an understudied dimension of Civil Rights Movement social history
key_limitations: Linda's exposure to canonical Civil Rights Movement events is largely secondhand through Millard and others; she repeatedly notes she was a supporter rather than a direct participant

Some dates and quantities are presented as approximate (Africa duration, distances, founding years), and the transcript contains likely ASR errors (e.g., 'Millard Fuller Farmer' for what is probably 'Millard Farmer'), which the interview itself does not resolve

claims.claim: Linda Fuller Degelmann was born in 1941 in Birmingham, Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was born in Birmingham, Alabama in 1941. (Segment 14)
claims.rationale: Linda states her birth year and city explicitly.

claims.claim: Linda was raised in Tuscaloosa, Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: When the war was over, we moved back to Alabama and settled in Tuscaloosa, Alabama. And that's where I was raised until I married in 1959. (Segments 22-23)
claims.rationale: Linda explicitly identifies Tuscaloosa as her place of upbringing.

claims.claim: Linda's parents operated Caldwell Electric Company on Broad Street in downtown Tuscaloosa.
claims.supporting_excerpt: After the war, he and my mother started business in downtown Tuscaloosa called Caldwell Electric Company. It was right on Broad Street. (Segments 25-26)
claims.rationale: The business name and Broad Street location are stated.

claims.claim: Linda married Millard (Dean) Fuller in 1959.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Like I said, I married in 1959. And my husband, Millard (Dean) Fuller... (Segments 53-54)
claims.rationale: Linda explicitly states marriage year and her husband's name.

claims.claim: Millard and his law-school partner Morris Dees set up a marketing business (Fuller & Dees Marketing) and a law practice in Montgomery.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He and a fellow law student had gone in business while they were in law school. And they set up a law practice in Montgomery. (Segments 72-73); ...their offices, Fuller & Dees Marketing, was broken into... (Segment 112)
claims.rationale: The business partnership and the name Fuller & Dees Marketing are explicitly stated.

claims.claim: Millard and Morris Dees attended Klan rallies to speak on behalf of MacDonald Gallion's run for Lieutenant Governor.
claims.supporting_excerpt: ...had spoken on behalf of the man running for Lieutenant Governor. And they went to Klan rallies to speak on behalf of MacDonald Gallion. (Segments 97-98)
claims.rationale: The candidate, role, and Klan rally venue are directly stated.

claims.claim: Millard and Morris received the Klan publication The Fiery Cross and later cancelled their subscription, with Morris placing a pistol on the desk.
claims.supporting_excerpt: ...they received the issues of what was called the The Fiery Cross. That was a publication... (Segment 100-101); ...they wanted to cancel their prescription to the The Fiery Cross. And Morris (Dees) kind of took a pistol out and put it on his desk. (Segments 108-109)
claims.rationale: The publication name, cancellation, and pistol details are explicitly described.

claims.claim: Millard and Morris witnessed Freedom Riders being beaten as they stepped off buses at the Montgomery bus station.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Millard (Dean) Fuller and Morris Dees actually went down to the bus station out of curiosity. Just to see what would happen when some of those buses came in. And they were shocked at how people were beat up as soon as they stepped off the bus. (Segments 85-87)
claims.rationale: The bus station observation and beatings are directly recounted.

claims.claim: The Fullers hosted Selma to Montgomery marchers from Ohio or Indiana in their home, drew nasty phone calls, and had their license plate photographed.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Some pastors that Millard (Dean) Fuller knew up in Ohio, Indiana... came down to participate. And they had a place to stay and they got in touch with Millard (Dean) Fuller... (Segments 121-123); ...our neighbors found out about it. We got some pretty nasty phone calls. (Segments 125-126); ...they can remember their license plate on the car, you know, a photograph of their license plate. (Segment 128)
claims.rationale: All elements (hosting, calls, photograph) are explicitly stated.

claims.claim: Linda attended Huntingdon College, a predominantly-white college, from 1961 to 1966.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was there beginning 1961 through 66. (Segment 185); I attended Huntingdon College, which was, I think, all white at that time. (Segment 74)
claims.rationale: Both college name, race composition, and date range are stated.

claims.claim: A 1965 marriage crisis prompted the Fullers to give away their wealth; Millard sold his half-interest to Morris Dees.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We actually left Millard (Dean) Fuller not left Montgomery in 1965. This is a whole other story about our marriage crisis. (Segments 138-139); ...sold my interest, half interest to my partner, Morris D's, who by the way started Southern Poverty Law Center. (Segment 151)
claims.rationale: All elements (1965 departure, marriage crisis, sale of interest to Morris Dees) are explicitly stated.

claims.claim: Morris Dees later founded the Southern Poverty Law Center, five or six years after the Fullers left Montgomery.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And that was five or six years later. He continued Fuller & Dees Marketing for a number of years before he started Southern Poverty Law Center. (Segment 152)
claims.rationale: Linda explicitly attributes SPLC's founding to Dees with the approximate 5-6-year timeline.

claims.claim: Dr. Durgan at Broadway United Church of Christ counseled Linda and was on the board of Tougaloo College.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So the pastor that I counseled with at Broadway United Church of Christ, Dr. Durgan, was on the board of Tougaloo College. (Segment 148); And so, Dr. Durgan said, well, I'm on the board for to glue. (Segment 153)
claims.rationale: Both pastor and board membership are stated.

claims.claim: The Fullers raised money for a Tougaloo College dormitory while living in New Jersey for two years (Montclair/Glen Ridge area).
claims.supporting_excerpt: And we actually settled our family in New Jersey for two years. (Segment 159); Well, as best as I can remember in Montclair and the Glen Ridge area, it was predominantly white. (Segment 165)
claims.rationale: Both the New Jersey duration and location are explicitly stated.

claims.claim: The Fullers met Clarence Jordan in December 1965 during a month-long visit to Koinonia Farm with Allen and Carol Henry.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So we decided to look Allen and Carol Henry up, Carol and, and Allen Henry. And that's when we first met Clarence. (Segments 780-781); ...so that was December, almost all of December of 1965. (Segment 785)
claims.rationale: Date and circumstances of first meeting are stated.

claims.claim: Allen Henry had been kicked out of his Birmingham, Alabama church for proposing welcoming Black people.
claims.supporting_excerpt: He had been kicked out of his church in Birmingham, Alabama for proposing, you know, they should be more welcoming to black people. (Segment 774)
claims.rationale: Reason for expulsion and city are stated.

claims.claim: The Fullers moved to Koinonia Farm in summer 1968.
claims.supporting_excerpt: When we moved to Koinonia Farm in the summer of 1968... (Segment 308)
claims.rationale: Both the date and destination are directly stated.

claims.claim: The Koinonia Partnership concept included partnership housing, partnership industries, and partnership farming.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And we would experiment with partnership, housing, partnership, industry, partnership farming... (Segments 341-343)
claims.rationale: All three partnership categories are stated.

claims.claim: Koinonia houses were built of cinder block with indoor bathrooms, running water, kitchen cabinets, and closets, sold at no profit and no interest.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And they were simple houses like the ones that we started off building or Cinder block. For the first time, families had an indoor bathroom running water, things we just take for granted. Closet, kitchen cabinets. (Segments 243-245); Sell them to the families, no profit, no interest... (Segment 242)
claims.rationale: All construction and financing terms are directly stated.

claims.claim: The Johnson family was the first family to move into a Koinonia partnership house.
claims.supporting_excerpt: You know, for the Johnson family was the first family to get to move in to one of the houses. (Segment 247)
claims.rationale: Family name and first-mover status are stated.

claims.claim: Linda set up a child development center at Koinonia using her elementary education degree.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So, since my degree was in elementary education, we set up a child development center there. (Segment 252)
claims.rationale: Both degree and center founding are stated.

claims.claim: Koinonia partnership industries included a pecan plant, sewing, and pottery.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Not only did we have the Peacond plant. And growing peanuts and corn. ... we started sewing industry and a pottery... (Segments 256-258)
claims.rationale: All three industries are listed (pecan transcribed as 'Peacond' - ASR artifact).

claims.claim: Koinonia Farm was founded by Clarence and Florence Jordan with Martin and Mabel England.
claims.supporting_excerpt: It was founded by Clarence and Florence Jordan. And Martin and Mabel England, who only stayed about two or three years, I guess. But they were there in the very beginning. (Segments 475-478)
claims.rationale: All four founders are named explicitly.

claims.claim: Clarence Jordan was a Greek New Testament scholar who studied at Southern Baptist Theological Seminary in Louisville, Kentucky.
claims.supporting_excerpt: ...he had studied about in seminary, it's Southern Baptist Theological Seminary in Louisville, Kentucky. (Segment 486); And then, of course, when he became a Greek New Testament scholar and read the original Greek... (Segments 495-496)
claims.rationale: Both seminary and Greek scholarship are stated.

claims.claim: Clarence Jordan grew up in Talbotton, Georgia and was a graduate of the University of Georgia in agriculture.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Talbotton, Georgia, he saw the discrimination. (Segment 489); And Clarence, being a graduate of the University of Georgia in agriculture... (Segment 481)
claims.rationale: Both hometown and educational background are stated.

claims.claim: In Unadilla, Georgia, about 30-40 miles east of Americus across Interstate 75, a Black teenager wanted to integrate the white high school.
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was a young man, a family, in Unadilla, Georgia. That's about 30, 40 miles east of here, just across the interstate, 75. (Segments 425-428); ...I wanted to get into the high school, the white high school. (Segment 435)
claims.rationale: Town, distance, interstate, and integration attempt are all stated.

claims.claim: The Fullers were arrested by a policeman in Unadilla while visiting the Black family's home with infant daughter Faith.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So Miller and I and Faith, little Faith, she was still in diapers. (Segments 442-443); And so I said to myself, I'm not going to let him take Miller off in the police car. And so I walked down and I said, if my husband was doing something wrong, I was too. You got to arrest me too. (Segments 602-604)
claims.rationale: Both the arrest and the family composition are stated.

claims.claim: Faith Fuller produced an hour-long documentary about Koinonia titled 'Briars in the Cotton Patch,' released in 2003.
claims.supporting_excerpt: This is an hour-long documentary entitled, Briars in the Cotton Patch, Our daughter, Faith Fuller, produced back in the early 2000. It was released in 2003... (Segments 449-452)
claims.rationale: Documentary title, length, and 2003 release date are explicitly stated.

claims.claim: On a December 1965 night service at First Baptist Church in Americus, the head deacon (also the town fire chief) ejected Collins McGee.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Anyway, along comes the head deacon, who happened to be the fire chief in town. And he comes in and he grabs hold of Collins McGee and he said, you got to get out of here. (Segments 812-813)
claims.rationale: Both the deacon's identity and the ejection are stated; the December 1965 timing is established earlier in the visit context (segment 785).

claims.claim: Clarence Jordan, Millard, Linda, Collins McGee (the only Black person in the group) and roughly 20 others attended the First Baptist service.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So we loaded up in several cars. They're probably about 20 of us. Clarence, Miller to me, Collins McGee. He was the only black person that would go with us. (Segments 795-798)
claims.rationale: Group composition is explicitly stated.

claims.claim: The Fullers spent three years in Africa (in what was then Zaire), where they built 120 houses.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We moved to Africa. We took our whole family to Africa for three years and pioneered the work there. (Segment 269); We came home after building about 120 houses. (Segment 273); When we lived there in the 70s, it was Zaire. (Segment 934)
claims.rationale: Duration, location, and number of houses are all stated.

claims.claim: Habitat for Humanity International was founded after the Fullers returned from Africa, when the new Koinonia leadership preferred to focus locally; the Fullers were given part of a chicken barn for an office.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And they said, no, we just want to build houses right here in Sumter County. We don't want to do this whole big worldwide thing. So they gave us part of a chicken barn. (Segments 285-287); And we eventually named what we were doing, Habitat for Humanity International. (Segment 289)
claims.rationale: All elements (founding context, chicken barn office, name) are directly stated.

claims.claim: Jimmy and Rosalynn Carter joined Habitat for Humanity in 1984.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Before the Carter's got involved, really, in 1984... (Segment 895)
claims.rationale: 1984 is the date Linda gives for Carter involvement.

claims.claim: By the time the Fullers left Habitat in 2005, it was active in 100 countries with over 900 employees and was receiving around a quarter of a million dollars per day.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And then, right before we left habitat in 2005, commonly a quarter of a million or more dollars would come in every day. Every day, we had work in a hundred countries. We had over 900 employees. (Segments 896-897)
claims.rationale: All numbers and the 2005 departure are stated.

claims.claim: After leaving Habitat in 2005, the Fullers founded the Fuller Center for Housing.
claims.supporting_excerpt: They've been involved, been involved with the effort that we started in 2005 after we left habitat, the Fuller Center for Housing. (Segment 1115)
claims.rationale: Both founding date and organization name are stated.

claims.claim: In 1971, a Black march and boycott in downtown Americus protested police brutality and the all-white police force, in which the Fullers participated.
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was, there was a march downtown America's and we actually participated in that as well as Millard (Dean) Fuller. (Segment 1006); ...black people just were bailed... we're not going to do any business in town. (Segments 1004-1005)
claims.rationale: Linda confirms participation in a downtown march and boycott. The 1971 date is taken from the interviewer's reference to Linda's biography (segment 997), not directly stated by Linda in the transcript, so the framing is supported but the specific year is interviewer-derived.

claims.claim: Linda mentioned that Andrew Young spoke about the Birmingham Children's Crusade of May 1963 and the moment marchers sat down before fire hoses were turned off.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I heard Andrew Young speak once, telling some of the stories. I can remember one in particular about the Birmingham Children's Crusade May 1963, and deciding, everybody just deciding to sit down... they eventually turned those hoses off. (Segments 1093-1095)
claims.rationale: Both Andrew Young as the speaker, the Birmingham Children's Crusade as the topic, the May 1963 date, and the sitting-down moment with hoses are all stated.

claims.claim: The Fullers founded a small Congregational/UCC church in their Montgomery home, later meeting at the Huntingdon College chapel and then a small building on the outskirts of Montgomery.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So we started in our home. And, you know, we built it up to about 20 people. And then we found we actually started meeting in the chapel of Huntingdon College. They let us use that on Sunday mornings for a while. And then we built a little building on the outskirts of Montgomery for a church out there. (Segments 982-984)
claims.rationale: All steps of the church history are stated.

claims.claim: Jimmy Carter persuaded the Fullers to join Maranatha Baptist Church.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And we didn't join another church until we joined Maranatha Baptist Church. Jimmy Carter twisted her arm. He said, okay, I've joined habitat now. I want you to join our church. (Segment 993)
claims.rationale: Both the Carter persuasion and the church name are stated.

claims.claim: Millard Fuller's law career later involved representing Black defendants in death-penalty cases, partnering with Atlanta lawyer Millard Farmer.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And Millard (Dean) Fuller just, Millard (Dean) Fuller figured out why or else in his, his partnering with people like Millard (Dean) Fuller Farmer in Atlanta, who was also doing a lot of death penalty cases. (Segment 1016)
claims.rationale: Linda mentions a partnering Atlanta lawyer doing death-penalty cases, but the transcript renders the name as 'Millard (Dean) Fuller Farmer' which appears to be an ASR error for 'Millard Farmer'. The claim is largely supported but the name attribution should be qualified.

claims.claim: Koinonia in the 1950s faced drive-by shootings, bullet holes in residences, and threats to Black families with house burnings.
claims.supporting_excerpt: In fact, at one point when, when cars were riding by and shooting into the farm... There were, there are still today some bullet holes that, in fact, the place where we were living, there was a bullet hole that came in and just barely miss one of the Jordan daughters. It shattered a mirror... (Segments 674-679); And the lady who worked in the candy kitchen at Koinonia Farm, Miss Gussie Jackson, this happened way before we moved there. But they threatened to burn her house down and they did. They set it on fire. (Segments 544-547)
claims.rationale: Both drive-by shootings and house burnings are directly recounted.

claims.claim: Conrad Browne was arrested for sharing a bag of peanuts with a Black Koinonia resident walking down the street in the 1950s.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I can remember hearing about a story of Conrad Browne was walking down the street with a guy. And they were sharing a bag of peanut. Walking with one of the black residents of Koinonia. And they were both arrested. (Segments 416-420)
claims.rationale: Linda recounts hearing the story; named person and arrest circumstances are stated.

publication_decision.rationale: The main_summary scored 86 on accuracy and 86 on quality, both below the 90/90 Smithsonian publication threshold. The citation audit found 40 supported claims, 2 partially_supported claims (the 1971 date for the Americus march, which is interviewer-derived rather than directly stated by Linda; and the 'Millard Farmer' name attribution which appears in the transcript as a likely-ASR-garbled 'Millard Fuller Farmer'). No claims were found to be unsupported. The interview is substantively faithful to the transcript, but the borderline scores and the two partially_supported claims correctly route this output to the human-review queue per the fail-closed publication gate. A human reviewer should resolve the ASR question about the Atlanta death-penalty lawyer's name and verify the year of the Americus boycott/march.
